items.title: Loan approval with two features
items.scenario: A bank uses income (x₁) and debt ratio (x₂) to approve (1) or deny (0) loans. A perceptron learns w₁, w₂, b so that approved applicants mostly satisfy w₁·income + w₂·debt + b ≥ 0. After training on 500 labeled cases, the boundary is a straight line in the income–debt plane.
items.explanation: Two real features → 2D hyperplane (a line). Works when good/bad loans are roughly separable by a linear rule; fails when high-income defaulters overlap low-risk applicants in a non-separable blob.

items.title: AND gate as a separable task
items.scenario: Boolean inputs (0,1) for x₁ and x₂. Labels: AND(x₁,x₂) = 1 only for (1,1). A perceptron with w = (1,1), b = −1.5 outputs 1 iff x₁ + x₂ − 1.5 ≥ 0, correctly implementing AND.
items.explanation: AND is linearly separable — classic proof that a single perceptron can learn simple logic gates before hitting XOR.

items.title: XOR failure motivates MLPs
items.scenario: Same binary inputs, but label XOR: 1 for (0,1) and (1,0), 0 for (0,0) and (1,1). No straight line separates the two classes. A single perceptron cycles or stalls; an MLP with a hidden layer can fold space and solve XOR.
items.explanation: Canonical non-separability example from Minsky & Papert — explains why depth (hidden layers) matters beyond one neuron.

items.title: Email spam keyword filter (historical)
items.scenario: Each email is a sparse bag-of-words vector x (1 if word present). A perceptron increments weights for spam words when misclassified: w ← w + η(y − ŷ)x. After many passes, large positive weights on words like "winner" and negative weights on "invoice" define a linear spam score with step output.
items.explanation: High-dimensional sparse text can still be linearly separable for coarse spam/ham — same linear model family as linear SVM and logistic regression, but with step loss instead of hinge or BCE.
